elements.sent-2411-001: Colonoscopy is crucial for identifying adenomatous polyps and preventing colorectal cancer.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, developing robust models for polyp detection is challenging by the limited size and accessibility of existing colonoscopy datasets.
elements.sent-2411-003: While previous efforts have attempted to synthesize colonoscopy images, current methods suffer from instability and insufficient data diversity.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, these approaches lack precise control over the generation process, resulting in images that fail to meet clinical quality standards.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address these challenges, we propose $CCIS-DIFF$, a $C$ontrolled generative model for high-quality $C$olonoscopy $I$mage $S$ynthesis based on a $Diff$usion architecture.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our method offers precise control over both the spatial attributes (polyp location and shape) and clinical characteristics of polyps that align with clinical descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, we introduce a blur mask weighting strategy to seamlessly blend synthesized polyps with the colonic mucosa, and a text-aware attention mechanism to guide the generated images to reflect clinical characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-008: Notably, to achieve this, we construct a new multi-modal colonoscopy dataset that integrates images, mask annotations, and corresponding clinical text descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental results demonstrate that our method generates high-quality, diverse colonoscopy images with fine control over both spatial constraints and clinical consistency, offering valuable support for downstream segmentation and diagnostic tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Colonoscopy is an essential tool for detecting adenomatous polyps and reducing rectal cancer mortality rates \cite{fan2020pranet,luo2024codeswap,ma2024generative .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, training models for automatic polyp detection is challenging due to the small scale of available colonoscopy datasets, making it difficult to have sufficient robustness and generalization that meet real-world clinical demands.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figure1.pdf} \caption{ In contrast to ArSDM \cite{du2023arsdm} and ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding}, CCIS-DIFF can utilize not only mask but also textual description to generate high-fidelity, text-consistent colonoscopy images. } \label{fig:figure1}
elements.sent-2411-012: To address this problem, previous methods \cite{ma2020cycle,xu2020ofgan,du2023arsdm,jeong2024uncertainty primarily relied on generative adversarial networks or diffusion models to synthesize more colonoscopy images.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Although these efforts aim to address the data scarcity problem, they struggle to generate a sufficiently diverse and high-quality image, and the generation process lacks adequate control, leading to images that fail to meet clinical requirements for practical use.
elements.sent-2411-014: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:figure1 , ArSDM \cite{du2023arsdm only utilizes the mask to synthesize the colonoscopy image and the generated image is of poor quality and contains noise.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Meanwhile, large-scale text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models such as Stable Diffusion \cite{rombach2022high and DALL·E \cite{ramesh2022hierarchical have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in generating images from various prompts.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This raises an important question: Can colonoscopy images be generated in a controlled manner using a pre-trained large-scale T2I model? In response, we present an innovative generative method to synthesize high-quality colonoscopy images in a controlled manner.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figure2.pdf} \caption{(a) The overall architecture of CCIS-DIFF. The parameters of the frozen modules refer to Stable Diffusion v1.5 \cite{rombach2022high}. The trainable modules incorporate a text-aware attention mechanism to address the issue of neglecting text prompts. Additionally, we introduce a blur mask weighting strategy to ensure seamless integration of synthesized polyps with the colonic mucosa. (b) The construction pipeline of the multi-modal colonoscopy dataset.} \label{fig:figure2}
elements.sent-2411-017: The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-018: We propose a novel generative model, named CCIS-DIFF, which offers fine control over both the spatial attributes and clinical characteristics of polyps, enabling more clinically consistent image synthesis for practical use.
elements.sent-2411-019: We introduce the blur mask weighting strategy to ensure seamless integration of synthesized polyps with the colonic mucosa, along with a text-aware attention mechanism that incorporates textual information into the generation process, to enable customization of polyp images.
elements.sent-2411-020: We design the first multi-modal colonoscopy dataset that uniquely combines colonoscopy images, segmentation masks, and clinical text descriptions, providing precise alignment between visual data and clinical information for improved training and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-021: A key challenge for current diffusion models in colonoscopy image synthesis \cite{du2023arsdm,jeong2024uncertainty is the absence of a dataset with consistent spatial characteristics of polyps and their corresponding clinical textual descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Existing datasets, such as EndoScene \cite{vazquez2017benchmark , CVC-ClinicDB \cite{bernal2015wm , and Kvasir \cite{jha2020kvasir , primarily contain image-mask pairs that focus on polyp regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, these datasets do not include detailed clinical text descriptions, limiting their usefulness for training models that aim to generate clinically accurate and diverse synthetic images.
elements.sent-2411-024: To address this limitation, we construct a novel multi-modal colonoscopy dataset that integrates three essential components: colonoscopy images, segmentation masks, and clinical text descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Such a dataset is vital for fine-tuning pretrained diffusion models, allowing them to adapt effectively to controlled colonoscopy image synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-026: An overview of our dataset construction process is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:figure2 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-027: To generate accurate captions that accurately reflect both spatial constraints and relevant clinical characteristics, we construct the open-source LLaMA \cite{touvron2023llama large language model (LLM) agent.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This agent takes a textual prompt and an image-mask colonoscopy pair to create captions for both the foreground and background.
elements.sent-2411-029: To increase textual diversity, the LLM incorporates different aspects like color, shape, texture, and swelling.
elements.sent-2411-030: As a result, we develop a multi-modal colonoscopy dataset consisting of triplets of colonoscopy images, mask images, and their corresponding textual descriptions which provides the necessary foundation for generating images with improved realism and variability.
elements.sent-2411-031: The overview architecture of our CCIS-DIFF is presented in Fig. \ref{fig:figure2 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Using our multi-modal colonoscopy dataset, we provide the original colonoscopy image $I$, the mask image $M$, and the corresponding text description $T$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Each of these components passes through its respective encoder, noting that the text encoder is frozen.
elements.sent-2411-034: Additionally, we develop a blur mask to ensure that the generated polyp integrates seamlessly with the background, the detailed description will be illustrated in Sec.. \ref{blur .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Based on ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding , we adopt the trainable diffusion branch and implement the zero convolution strategy to protect this branch by eliminating random noise as gradients in the initial training steps.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This structure, when applied to large models like Stable Diffusion \cite{rombach2022high , enables the frozen parameters to preserve the integrity of the production-ready model that has been trained on billions of images.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Meanwhile, the trainable diffusion branch leverages this large-scale pre-trained model to establish a robust backbone for managing multi-modal input conditions.
elements.sent-2411-038: Furthermore, to address the issue of neglecting text prompt and effectively incorporating textual information into the generation process, we incorporate a text-aware attention mechanism (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-039: The purpose of the blur mask weighting strategy is to ensure that the generated polyp is seamlessly integrated with the background.
elements.sent-2411-040: To achieve this, we apply a Gaussian blur operation $\sigma$ to the mask image $M$, softening the transition between the polyp mask region and the background.
elements.sent-2411-041: We utilize two separate mask encoders to extract features from the mask and the blurred mask, each with non-shared parameters.
elements.sent-2411-042: Subsequently, a weighting matrix $M_w$ is introduced to balance the two mask branches, and $M_w$ is learned using a three-layer MLP.
elements.sent-2411-043: Thus the blur mask embedding $F_b$ can be constructed as: \begin{equation}F_b = M_w \odot E_m (\sigma(M)),\end{equation} where $\odot$ is the Hadamard product and $E_m$ denotes the mask encoder.
elements.sent-2411-044: In our experiments, we observed that existing methods, such as ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding , often overlook the text prompt and rely more heavily on the mask image.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We hypothesize that this visual dominance over the text prompt arises from the text-free nature of the self-attention layers.
elements.sent-2411-046: To address this issue, we propose a text-aware attention mechanism that leverages the cross-attention matrix to regulate the output of the self-attention.
elements.sent-2411-047: Given the noise embedding $F_n$, it is first passed through a ResNet block, as referenced in ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Following this, we obtain the attention input tensor $F_a \in \mathbb{R ^{(h \times w) \times d}$, which is processed through projection layers to derive the queries $Q_s$, keys $K_s$, and values $V_s$, as well as the attention map $M_{s = \frac{Q_s K_s^{T {\sqrt{d \in \mathbb{R ^{hw\times hw}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: To reduce the strong influence of the mask image, we adjust the attention scores of the prompt text.
elements.sent-2411-050: Specifically, we begin by constructing the cross-attention similarity matrix: \begin{equation}M_{c}=SoftMax(Q_{c}K_{c}^{T}/\sqrt{d}),\end{equation} where $Q_{c \in \mathbb{R ^{(h\times w)\times d , K_{c \in \mathbb{R ^{l\times d}$ represent the query and key tensors from their corresponding cross-attention layers, and $l$ denotes the number of tokens in the text prompt.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: For each pixel $j$, we define its similarity to the text prompt by summing its similarity scores with the embedding indexed by $M_c$.
elements.sent-2411-052: We then apply a clamp operation to normalize the scores $s_j$ within the range $\left[0,1\right]$: \begin{equation}s_j = Norm (Sum_{j}(M_c)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: By calculating the scores for each pixel, we can obtain the final text-aware map $S$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Subsequently, we utilize $S$ to compute the updated attention map and refine the self-attention process.
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, the output of the cross-attention mechanism interacts once more with the blur mask embedding, generating the final output embedding through a ResNet block.
elements.sent-2411-056: During the training phase, the diffusion models start with an original image $I$ and progressively add noise to create a noisy embedding $I_t$, where $t$ indicates the number of times noise is added.
elements.sent-2411-057: The models learn a network $\epsilon_{\theta}$ to predict the noise added to the noisy image $I_t$, based on a set of conditions that include the time step $t$, a text prompt $T$, and a mask image $M$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The overall loss function $\mathcal{L}$ for the entire diffusion model is represented as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}=\mathbb{E}_{I,t,T,M,\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)}\Big[\|\epsilon-\epsilon_{\theta}(I_{t},{t},T,M))\|_{2}^{2}\Big].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, we randomly replace half of the text prompts with empty strings.
elements.sent-2411-060: This strategy enhances the model's ability to directly recognize the semantics of the input mask image, serving as a substitute for the text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-061: During the inference phase, the diffusion models automatically generate noise and produce the final colonoscopy images based on a text prompt and a mask image.
elements.sent-2411-062: Simultaneously, we utilize Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) \cite{ho2022classifier to enhance the sampling process.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{The quantitative results of the colonoscopy image synthesis. The boldface indicates the best performance.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lccc} Method FID $\downarrow$ CLIP-score $\uparrow$ CLIP-image $\uparrow$ ControlNet 87.32 30.78 87.09 Uni-ControlNet 92.74 30.57 86.46 Ours 71.73 31.96 88.70 \end{tabular} } \label{table1}
elements.sent-2411-063: All methods were implemented in PyTorch on a single NVIDIA A100 40G GPU.
elements.sent-2411-064: We utilized the pre-trained Stable Diffusion v1.5 \cite{rombach2022high model, following ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding , to replicate its UNet encoder as the trainable copy encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The batch size was configured to 4, and the learning rate was set to 1e-5.
elements.sent-2411-066: For inference, we used a default CFG scale of 7.0.
elements.sent-2411-067: The DDIM sampler was employed, using 20 steps to sample each image.
elements.sent-2411-068: For the colonoscopy image synthesis task, we compared our method with ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding and Uni-ControlNet \cite{zhao2024uni , all of which were trained on our multi-modal colonoscopy dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Following \cite{zhang2023adding , we assessed performance using FID, CLIP-score, and CLIP-image (measuring the similarity between the generated image and the reference image).}
elements.sent-2411-070: For the downstream polyp segmentation task, we utilized PraNet \cite{fan2020pranet and Polyp-PVT \cite{dong2021polyp as baseline segmentation models with their default settings, and assessed their performance using mean Dice (mDice) and mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) metrics.}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{User study. The rating scale ranges from 1 to 5, with higher numbers indicating better performance.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lccc} Method Image Fidelity Mask Accuracy Text Accuracy ControlNet 3.852 3.962 3.918 Uni-ControlNet 2.904 2.966 3.392 Ours 4.266 4.466 4.518 \end{tabular} } \label{table2}
elements.figure-2411-006: [] \caption{Comparisons of different settings applied on two polyp segmentation baselines \cite{fan2020pranet,dong2021polyp} across three public datasets \cite{vazquez2017benchmark,bernal2015wm,jha2020kvasir}.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}lcccccc@{}} \multicolumn{2}{c}{EndoScene} \multicolumn{2}{c}{CVC-ClinicDB} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Kvasir} \cmidrule(l){2-7} Settings mDice mIoU mDice mIoU mDice mIoU PraNet 86.1 79.1 90.8 86.1 89.2 84.0 PraNet + ControlNet 85.4 77.7 90.1 85.1 90.6 85.2 PraNet + Uni-ControlNet 85.9 78.2 89.5 85.1 87.9 82.5 PraNet + Ours 88.5 81.0 92.8 88.0 92.8 87.1 PVT 88.2 81.2 92.8 87.6 91.5 86.8 PVT + ControlNet 88.3 81.4 92.7 88.2 90.3 84.8 PVT + Uni-ControlNet 85.8 79.6 89.3 83.8 86.5 80.7 PVT + Ours 89.3 82.1 93.5 88.4 92.4 87.0 \end{tabular} } \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.25cm} \label{table3}
elements.sent-2411-071: The quantitative results of the colonoscopy image synthesis are illustrated in Table \ref{table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Our method outperforms others in both visual quality and text alignment.
elements.sent-2411-073: Furthermore, we present a comparison of our method with others in Fig. \ref{fig:figure3 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: The results show that the polyp region generated by our method is more consistent with the mask image, and the overall information aligns more closely with the text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-075: To conduct a more comprehensive evaluation, we implemented a user study questionnaire.
elements.sent-2411-076: We selected 30 groups of images for comparison and invited 15 clinical experts from the First Affiliated Hospital of Sun Yat-sen University to participate in the survey.
elements.sent-2411-077: Participants were required to rate the synthesized images based on three criteria: image fidelity, mask accuracy, and text accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-078: The average scores for each criterion are presented in Table \ref{table2 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Our method outperforms the other methods in all aspects.
elements.sent-2411-080: To further verify the effectiveness of our synthetic images, we evaluated our method against state-of-the-art methods for the polyp segmentation task.
elements.sent-2411-081: We generated the same number of samples as the diffusion training set using the original masks, which we then combined to create a new downstream training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-082: The experimental results presented in Table \ref{table3 highlight the effectiveness of our method in training improved downstream models that achieve superior performance.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We also conduct ablation visualization experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the Blur Mask Weighting Strategy (BMWS) and the Text-Aware Attention Mechanism (TAAM).
elements.sent-2411-084: The results are illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:figure4 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: The combination of BMWS and TAAM significantly enhances the quality of the synthesized images and text alignment.
elements.sent-2411-086: In this paper, we present CCIS-DIFF, a generative model that leverages a Stable Diffusion prior for the controlled colonoscopy image synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-087: We begin by developing a blur mask weighting strategy to seamlessly integrate the generated polyp with the colonic mucosa, along with a text-aware attention mechanism to address the issue of neglecting text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-088: Additionally, we introduce a multi-modal colonoscopy dataset created by large language models.
elements.sent-2411-089: Extensive experiments across various settings demonstrate the superior performance of CCIS-DIFF.
elements.sent-2411-090: This research study was conducted retrospectively using human subject data made available in open access.
elements.sent-2411-091: Ethical approval was not required as confirmed by the license attached with the open access data.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEbib.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Colonoscopy is an essential tool for detecting adenomatous polyps and reducing rectal cancer mortality rates \cite{fan2020pranet .}

The parameters of the frozen modules refer to Stable Diffusion v1.5 \cite{rombach2022high}. The trainable modules incorporate a text-aware attention mechanism to address the issue of neglecting text prompts. Additionally, we introduce a blur mask weighting strategy to ensure seamless integration of synthesized polyps with the colonic mucosa. (b) The construction pipeline of the multi-modal colonoscopy dataset.} \label{fig:figure2}
elements.sent-2411-093: To address this limitation, we built a new multi-modal colonoscopy dataset based on existing datasets, containing three key components: colonoscopy images, segmentation masks, and clinical text descriptions.
elements.figure-2411-010: [] \caption{Comparisons of different settings applied on two polyp segmentation baselines across three public datasets.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}lcccccc@{}} \multicolumn{2}{c}{EndoScene} \multicolumn{2}{c}{CVC-ClinicDB} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Kvasir} \cmidrule(l){2-7} Settings mDice mIoU mDice mIoU mDice mIoU PraNet 86.1 79.1 90.8 86.1 89.2 84.0 PraNet + ControlNet 85.4 77.7 90.1 85.1 90.6 85.2 PraNet + Uni-ControlNet 85.9 78.2 89.5 85.1 87.9 82.5 PraNet + Ours 88.5 81.0 92.8 88.0 92.8 87.1 PVT 88.2 81.2 92.8 87.6 91.5 86.8 PVT + ControlNet 88.3 81.4 92.7 88.2 90.3 84.8 PVT + Uni-ControlNet 85.8 79.6 89.3 83.8 86.5 80.7 PVT + Ours 89.3 82.1 93.5 88.4 92.4 87.0 \end{tabular} } \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.25cm} \label{table3}
